title: 🎙️ Science, Wonder & the Natural World
description: Today we look at scientific discovery and the natural world — from ecology and wildlife research to astronomy and deep-sea biology. We're grounding these stories in the landscapes and living systems of the Cariboo: what salmon runs, boreal forest research, or species-at-risk studies tell us about the wild places we live beside. We're after stories that inspire curiosity and wonder about how the world works. - Daily themed article selection for podcast generation

_podcast.theme_description: Today we look at scientific discovery and the natural world — from ecology and wildlife research to astronomy and deep-sea biology. We're grounding these stories in the landscapes and living systems of the Cariboo: what salmon runs, boreal forest research, or species-at-risk studies tell us about the wild places we live beside. We're after stories that inspire curiosity and wonder about how the world works.
_podcast.theme_scoring_prompt: Rate how well this article fits the theme 'Science, Wonder & the Natural World' — scientific discovery, ecology, natural phenomena, and the curiosity-driven study of living systems. Strong fits include: wildlife biology and species research, ecology studies and biodiversity findings, watershed and salmon research relevant to BC, astronomy and space science, new species discoveries, citizen science projects, natural history stories, wildfire science and forest ecology, climate science findings (not just policy), geology, evolution, and deep-time Earth stories, and any scientific research grounded in BC or Cariboo landscapes and ecosystems. Prefer articles that convey genuine curiosity and discovery — the 'how' and 'why' of the natural world. Score 70–90 for: prehistoric life and palaeontology (ancient oceans, Cretaceous ecology, deep-time species), new species discoveries in ocean or remote environments, deep-sea biology, evolutionary biology findings, natural history stories with a strong sense of wonder, and field research grounded in BC ecosystems. Penalise (score 0–30): consumer health and pharmaceutical research without ecology or natural-world context; pure policy and advocacy news; technology business stories without a science or ecology angle; human interest and viral news without scientific substance; geopolitical and crime news. Score 0-100 where 100 is a perfect thematic match and 0 is completely unrelated.

items.title: [ScienceDaily] Earth’s waters are quietly running out of oxygen, scientists warn
items.content_html:
🔭 ✏️

Oxygen is disappearing from oceans, lakes, rivers, and coastal waters at an alarming rate, threatening aquatic life and weakening the natural processes that help regulate Earth’s climate. Scientists warn that this widespread deoxygenation is closely connected to other major planetary threats, including warming, pollution, biodiversity loss, and ocean acidification.
items.summary: Oxygen is disappearing from oceans, lakes, rivers, and coastal waters at an alarming rate, threatening aquatic life and weakening the natural processes that help regulate Earth’s climate. Scientists warn that this widespread deoxygenation is closely connected to other major planetary threats,

items.title: [ScienceDaily] Scientists discover a protein that protects the brain from Alzheimer’s damage
items.content_html:
🔭 ✏️

A protein called SORLA may act as a powerful defense against the toxic tau tangles involved in Alzheimer’s disease. Mice with extra SORLA had less tau accumulation, less brain atrophy, and healthier connections between neurons, while mice without it experienced more severe damage. The findings also revealed a possible drug target that could help control harmful activity in supporting brain cells.
items.summary: A protein called SORLA may act as a powerful defense against the toxic tau tangles involved in Alzheimer’s disease. items.title: [Boing Boing] Rising CO2 may already be showing up in human blood  — a "potentially toxic atmosphere in 50 years"
items.content_html:
🔭 ✏️

For most of human evolution, the air held about 300 parts per million of carbon dioxide. It has now surpassed 420 and is climbing by more than 2 ppm per year. A new open-access study in Air Quality, Atmosphere & Health argues that shift may already be showing up in our blood. — Read the rest

The post Rising CO2 may already be showing up in human blood — a "potentially toxic atmosphere in 50 years" appeared first on Boing Boing.

items.title: [Boing Boing] The $11 million mummy that turned out to be a fake
items.content_html:
🔭 ✏️

In October 2000, a stunning discovery hit the antiquities market in Pakistan: a mummy in a gilded coffin, wearing a golden crown, with a cuneiform inscription identifying her as Rhodugune, a daughter of the Persian king Xerxes. Experts dated the Persian Princess to around 600 BC, and Iran, Pakistan, and the Taliban each rushed to claim her. — Read the rest

The post The $11 million mummy that turned out to be a fake appeared first on Boing Boing.

items.title: [New Atlas] Robotic elephant-trunk gripper uses internal camera for a sense of touch
items.content_html:
🔭 ✏️

Evolution has already found solutions to lots of problems, so engineers more often borrow ideas from nature instead of reinventing the wheel. We’ve previously seen robots that can curl up like armadillos to protect themselves, and now researchers have developed yet another soft robotic gripper that mimics the tip of an elephant’s trunk. It’s called EleTac and, according to its creators, can actually “feel” the objects it touches.

Continue Reading

Category: Robotics, Engineering

Tags: Japan Advanced Institute of Science and Technology, Biomimicry, Elephant, Touch

items.title: [Atlas Obscura] Monument to the Hippies in Ibiza, Spain
items.content_html:
🔭 ✏️

This sculpture by Ció Abellí shows a father, with long hair and a typical hippie appearance. He walks with his little daughter who looks the same way. At their feet is a world map with the places in the world where the hippie communities were concentrated: San Francisco, Amsterdam, Kathmandu, Goa, and Ibiza. The monument pays tribute to the hippie movement that has played an important role in the development of Ibiza.

The bronze sculpture group is the three-dimensional materialization of an image taken in 1973 by photographer Toni Riera. You might think that it reflects a scene that took place in Ibiza, but it actually happened in a park in Amsterdam.

Riera was only 18 years old, and one weekend in Vondelpark, he saw two strangers: a father and his daughter. He snapped a photo that would become his best-known work, though he didn't know it at the time. During that time, Toni Riera first traveled to Ibiza and started working at Pachá washing glasses, and Ricardo Urgell, the owner of this famous nightclub, hired him as a photographer. On the walls of Pachá, he portrayed his version of some of the most important characters of the 20th century.

In the 90s, Urgell wanted to relaunch the place and give it an appearance more typical of the island's beginnings, as the hippie community that it once was in the 70s, and asked Riera for a photo that represented that image. The photographer searched through his negatives and found that old photo from 20 years ago taken in Amsterdam. Needless to say, that image of Pachá would end up becoming an icon of hippie Ibiza and at the same time meant a relaunch of the famous nightclub. In this way, a grateful Urgell commissioned the sculptor Ció Abellí to create a bronze statue that represented the photograph, and after that he donated this sculpture to the city of Ibiza, where, since its inauguration in 2016, we can see it immortalized in the port of the Ibizan capital.
items.summary: This sculpture by Ció Abellí shows a father, with long hair and a typical hippie appearance. He walks with his little daughter who looks the same way. At their feet is a world map with the places in the world where the hippie communities were concentrated: San Francisco, Amsterdam, Kathmandu, Goa,

At their feet is a world map with the places in the world where the hippie communities were concentrated: San Francisco, Amsterdam, Kathmandu, Goa, and Ibiza. The monument pays tribute to the hippie movement that has played an important role in the development of Ibiza . items.title: [Atlas Obscura] Box Hill War Memorial in Box Hill, Australia
items.content_html:
🔭 ✏️

The sight and sound of the bugle, a simple brass instrument, is one that many associate with remembrance. This memorial, in the middle of Box Hill Park, acts as a poignant symbol to remember local lives lost in World War I. 

The monument was erected in 1922 and unveiled by the local Governor-General Lord Forster. The names of those who lost their lives during the war are commemorated on a plaque, all listed individually. The marble soldier stands holding a bugle to his lips and sits on a granite column. It was erected by the Council of the Shire of Nunawading. 

A wall has been erected in the area surrounding the memorial, and on this wall now lie several plaques that pay tribute to lives lost in other wars in which Australian citizens have served. 

There is also another sculpture at the site that is equally as poignant and pays tribute to lives lost in World War II. This depicts a soldier carrying a wounded comrade on his back and has the word 'Kokoda Trail' written underneath. This symbolizes the Kokoda Trail campaign, part of the Pacific War of WWII, which consisted of several battles fought from July to November 1942 in the then Australian Territory of Papua.
items.summary: The sight and sound of the bugle, a simple brass instrument, is one that many associate with remembrance. This memorial, in the middle of Box Hill Park, acts as a poignant symbol to remember local lives lost in World War I. The monument was erected in 1922 and unveiled by the local Governor-General

items.title: [Atlas Obscura] Umtanum Creek Footbridge in Ellensburg, Washington
items.content_html:
🔭 ✏️

Yakima (pronounced "YAK-uh-maw") Canyon, where the Yakima River traverses a water gap through Manastash Ridge, is an obvious thoroughfare and was used by Native Americans for thousands of years, and then by Euro-American trappers and settlers into the 19th century. It was also an obvious route for a railroad, and the Northern Pacific Railway was built through it in the early 1880s, along the west bank of the river. (The railroad is still in active use and is now part of the BNSF system.)

Railroading was an extremely labor-intensive business well into the 20th century, and camps were built along the right-of-way to house the trackwalkers, signal men, section gangs, and other workers. In 1885 one such camp was built here at Umtanum Creek, where the eponymous creek joins the Yakima. The camp even boasted a school and a post office by the early 20th century.

When in 1925 the automobile highway was built through the canyon on the east shore of the river, cable suspension bridges were built across the river from the railroad camps to provide highway access. Evidently the original bridges were wide enough to be passable to Ford Model As. In the 1960s, the Washington Department of Fish and Wildlife and the Bureau of Land Management took over the day-to-day management of the bridge, but only in 1987 was the bridge deck narrowed to make it accessible to pedestrians only.

With the continuing rise of automotive travel, it proved no longer necessary to station crews on the railroad right-of-way, and by the 1970s the last of the camps had been removed.

The cable suspension bridges were also removed, with the sole exception of the Umtanum Creek bridge. It was still used by recreational users to access the west side of the river, but because of age-related deterioration concerns were being raised about its safety. Meanwhile, efforts were underway to have the bridge declared a national historic site. Finally, Umtanum Creek Bridge was restored in early 2021, with attention paid to preserving the structure's historic values.
items.summary: Yakima (pronounced "YAK-uh-maw") Canyon, where the Yakima River traverses a water gap through Manastash Ridge, is an obvious thoroughfare and was used by Native Americans for thousands of years, and then by Euro-American trappers and settlers into the 19th century. It was also an obvious route for

items.title: [Kottke.org] Goofy Oversized Wicker Costumes
items.content_html:
🔭 ✏️

Artist Lewis Prosser describes himself on Instagram as an “absurdist basket-maker”. His costumes for a project called Making Merrie reflect that self-description.

Making Merrie explores the material culture of folk theatre. Inspired by mummers’ plays and masked traditions along the Wales/England border, Making Merrie combines craft, performance, and language to reflect on cultural heritage and exchange.

Mummers’ plays are traditional folk performances with roots over 500 years old, often tied to Christmas and New Year. Full of humour and spontaneous revelry, these plays were staged in streets, homes, or pubs by amateur troupes, telling simple stories of combat, death, and miraculous revival. Unlike the religious Mystery Plays, mummers’ plays are secular, carnivalesque, and performed for community fun.

The project features large-scale wicker costumes, handcrafted using regional willow basketry techniques, highlighting basketry as an essential human skill we’re at risk of forgetting—a skill that, if lost, means losing part of what it is to be human.

(via colossal)

Tags: art · Lewis Prosser
items.summary: Artist Lewis Prosser describes himself on Instagram as an “absurdist basket-maker”. His costumes for a project called Making Merrie reflect that self-description. Making Merrie explores the material culture of folk theatre. Inspired by mummers’ plays and masked traditions along the Wales/England
items._excerpt: Artist Lewis Prosser describes himself on Instagram as an “absurdist basket-maker”. His costumes for a project called Making Merrie reflect that self-description. Making Merrie explores the material culture of folk theatre. Inspired by mummers’ plays and masked traditions along the Wales/England border, Making Merrie combines craft, performance, and language to reflect on cultural heritage and exchange. Mummers’ plays are traditional folk performances with roots over 500 years old, often tied to Christmas and New Year. Full of humour and spontaneous revelry, these plays were staged in

items.title: [ScienceDaily] Earth has a natural thermostat and scientists finally know how it works
items.content_html:
🔭 ✏️

Scientists have identified a hidden feedback loop that may explain how Earth has regulated its climate for tens of millions of years. As sea levels rose and fell, they changed how much phosphate reached the open ocean, affecting marine life and the amount of carbon buried beneath the seafloor. That burial removed carbon dioxide from the atmosphere, helping cool the planet.

items.title: [Kagi Small Web] EU against Meta
items.content_html:
🔭 ✏️

* EU regulators have accused Meta, the company behind Facebook and Instagram, of failing to tackle the risks of its "addictive design" on the physical and mental health of users.*

This is a very important problem, and one of the reasons to refuse to use those systems. But if the EU is serious about protecting users from them, it should take firm action -- take a big bite out of the problem, not a little nibble. Use of a recommendation engine should not be enabled by default.

I think that a platform should not be allowed to use or suggest any particular recommedation engine.
